content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 30, 1990




Elizabeth H. Silver
Meyers, Nave, Riback & West
777 Davis Street Suite 300
San Leandro, CA 94577

                                          Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                               Our File No. I-90-644

Dear Ms. Silver:

     This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Dublin Planning Commissioner Carl Springer regarding his duties
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1  Because your request does not concern a
specific governmental decision, we will provide informal advice.2
Please note that we do not provide advice concerning provisions of
law other than the Act.  You may wish to consult with the Attorney
General's office regarding other provisions of law such as Section
1090 which prohibits government officials from having an interest
in contracts with their agencies.

                              QUESTION

     As a planning commissioner, may Mr. Springer participate in
decisions where either he or his employer has prepared a traffic
analysis or some other report for the city?




_________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-644
Page 2

CONCLUSION

Mr. Springer may not make, participate in making, or attempt
to influence a governmental decision that will have a material
financial effect on TJKM, his source of income. He may prepare
traffic reports if he does not participate in a decision regarding
the report and has no contact with the agency regarding the report
except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the report.

FACTS

Carl Springer is a member of the Dublin Planning Commission
and works for a private firm, TJKM, as a traffic engineer. TJKM
has a contract with the city and is compensated on an hourly
basis. Under the contract, TJKM (1) prepares traffic analyses for
city staff's use in recommending the imposition of traffic impact
fees on applicants, (b) prepares traffic analyses for use in
preparation of environmental documents, and (c) responds to
planning staff inquiries regarding traffic impacts. Initially Mr.
Springer will not work on any Dublin projects, but it is possible
his employer will assign a Dublin project to him at a later date.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides that no public official shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest. An official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on any source of income
aggregating $250 or more provided to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section
87103(c).) Mr. Springer apparently has received over $250 in
income from TJKM within the preceding 12 months, and accordingly
TJKM constitutes a source of income. (Section 82030.)³

A member of a planning commission is a public official.
(Section 82048.) A public official "makes a governmental
decision" when he or she votes on a matter, obligates the agency
to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.) A
public official "participates in the making of a governmental
decision" when he or she advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part

_______________
³ Other subdivisions of 87103 define financial interest based on
an interest in a business entity or in real property. From your
letter, it appears Mr. Springer has no proprietary interest in
TJKM but only receives a salary. Please advise if the facts are
otherwise.

Our File No. I-90-644
Page 3

of the official, or preparing or presenting any report, analysis
or opinion, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official. (Regulation 18700(c), copy enclosed.) An official
"attempts to use his or her official position to influence a
decision" if the official contacts, appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant
of the agency.

Mr. Springer would be required to disqualify himself if it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision would have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on TJKM. The effect of a decision is "reasonably
foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will
occur. Certainty is not required; however, if an effect is a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198, copy enclosed.)

"Materiality" is defined by Regulations 18702-18702.6.
First, it must be determined whether the source of income, TJKM,
is "directly involved" in the decision or whether a nexus exists
between the decision and the purpose for which the income was
received. Regulation 18702.1(b) (copy enclosed) defines "directly
involved":

     A person or business entity is directly
involved in a decision before an official's agency
when that person or entity, either personally or by
an agent:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

                                    Regulation 18702.1(b)

     If a source of income is directly involved in the decision,
the financial effect of the decision on the source is deemed
material. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).) Accordingly, Mr. Springer
may not participate in a decision concerning such matters as the
renewal or expansion of TJKM's contract. Moreover, if a report
prepared by TJKM is submitted before the commission for decision,
alone or in conjunction with other matters, TJKM may be "directly
involved" in the decision depending on the nature of its contract
with the city. If the contract is not an ongoing contract, or is
such that the city has the right to cancel the contract at any

Our File No. I-90-644
Page 4
time, the planning commission would be impliedly considering the
renewal or revocation of the contract each time a TJKM report is
presented to it. (See McHugh Advice Letter, No. 89-443, copy
enclosed; Regulation 18702.1(b)(3).) Without more specifics
regarding the nature of the contract, we cannot determine whether
TJKM would be "directly involved" when one of its reports is
before the commission.

Even if TJKM is not "directly involved," Mr. Springer must
disqualify himself if there is a nexus between the decision and
the purpose for which he receives income from TJKM. A "nexus"
exists if the official receives income to achieve a goal or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision. In other words, the official may not accomplish in
his public capacity what he is paid to accomplish in his private
capacity. (Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152, Sprague Advice
Letter, No. I-88-190, Chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-091, Curry
Advice Letter, No. I-87-300, Goldstein Advice Letter, No. I-87-
184, copies enclosed.) For example, if Mr. Springer prepares a
traffic analysis that is presented to the commission, he may not
vote on the matter because one of the purposes of his being paid
by TJKM is to prepare traffic analyses that are acceptable to the
planning commission. (See Shaw Advice Letter, A-87-114, copy
enclosed.) Nor may he vote on a co-worker's report, because
typically an employee is paid to promote the employer's interests,
and it would be against the employer's interest to have any of its
projects rejected. (See Suess Advice Letter, No. A-88-468, copy
enclosed.)

Finally, Mr. Springer may have to disqualify himself if TJKM
is indirectly involved in a decision before the commission. For
example, this might occur if another party appeals before the
commission and an issue arises regarding the effect of the
decision on traffic, and the commission is considering ordering a
traffic report. If, under the circumstances, it is reasonably
foreseeable that TJKM will be awarded additional work because of
the decision, Mr. Springer should not participate in the decision
if the materiality tests of Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) are
met.

The exceptions contained in Regulation 18700.1(b)(4) would
allow Mr. Springer to work on projects for Dublin. For the
reasons stated above, as a commissioner he would not be able to
make or participate in making a decision concerning his own work.
Moreover, he may not have any contact with the agency regarding
his work except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning
the processing or evaluation of the report. Please review the
Holbert advice letter (Advice Letter No. I 90-080, copy enclosed)
for details regarding permissible communication with city staff.

Our File No. I-90-644
Page 5

     I trust this letter adequately addresses your questions.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  Peggy Bernardy
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:dg

Enclosures

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

He may prepare traffic reports if he does not participate in a decision regarding the report and has no contact with the agency regarding the report except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the report.

TJKM has a contract with the city and is compensated on an hourly basis. Under the contract, TJKM (1) prepares traffic analyses for city staff's use in recommending the imposition of traffic impact fees on applicants, (b) prepares traffic analyses for use in preparation of environmental documents, and (c) responds to planning staff inquiries regarding traffic impacts. Initially Mr. Springer will not work on any Dublin projects, but it is possible his employer will assign a Dublin project to him at a later date.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 provides that no public official shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. An official has a financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any source of income aggregating $250 or more provided to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) Mr. Springer apparently has received over $250 in income from TJKM within the preceding 12 months, and accordingly TJKM constitutes a source of income. (Section 82030.)3

A member of a planning commission is a public official. (Section 82048.) A public official "makes a governmental decision" when he or she votes on a matter, obligates the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.) A public official "participates in the making of a governmental decision" when he or she advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part

3 Other subdivisions of 87103 define financial interest based on an interest in a business entity or in real property. From your letter, it appears Mr. Springer has no proprietary interest in TJKM but only receives a salary. Please advise if the facts are otherwise.

of the official, or preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official. (Regulation 18700(c), copy enclosed.) An official "attempts to use his or her official position to influence a decision" if the official contacts, appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency.

Mr. Springer would be required to disqualify himself if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision would have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on TJKM. The effect of a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required; however, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198, copy enclosed.)

"Materiality" is defined by Regulations 18702-18702.6. First, it must be determined whether the source of income, TJKM, is "directly involved" in the decision or whether a nexus exists between the decision and the purpose for which the income was received. Regulation 18702.1(b) (copy enclosed) defines "directly involved":

A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.

(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b)

If a source of income is directly involved in the decision, the financial effect of the decision on the source is deemed material. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).) Accordingly, Mr. Springer may not participate in a decision concerning such matters as the renewal or expansion of TJKM's contract. Moreover, if a report prepared by TJKM is before the commission for a decision, whether alone or in conjunction with other matters, TJKM may be "directly involved" in the decision depending on the nature of its contract with the city. If the contract is not an ongoing contract, or is such that the city has the right to cancel the contract at any

time, the planning commission would be impliedly considering the renewal or revocation of the contract each time a TJKM report is presented to it. (See McHugh Advice Letter, No. 89-443, copy enclosed; Regulation 18702.1(b)(3).) Without more specifics regarding the nature of the contract, we cannot determine whether TJKM would be "directly involved" when one of its reports is before the commission.

Even if TJKM is not "directly involved," Mr. Springer must disqualify himself if there is a nexus between the decision and the purpose for which he receives income from TJKM. "Nexus" exists if the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision. In other words, the official may not accomplish in his public capacity what he is paid to accomplish in his private capacity. (Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152, Sprague Advice Letter, No. I-88-190, Chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-091, Curry Advice Letter, No. I-87-300, Goldstein Advice Letter, No. I-87-184, copies enclosed.) For example, if Mr. Springer prepares a traffic analysis that is presented to the commission, he may not vote on the matter because one of the purposes of his being paid by TJKM is to prepare traffic analyses that are acceptable to the planning commission. (See Shaw Advice Letter, A 87-114, copy enclosed.) Nor may he vote on a co-worker's report, because typically an employee is paid to promote the employer's interests, and it would be against the employer's interest to have any of its projects rejected. (See Suess Advice Letter, No. A-88-468, copy enclosed.)

Finally, Mr. Springer may have to disqualify himself if TJKM is indirectly involved in a decision before the commission. For example, this might occur if another party appears before the commission and an issue arises regarding the effect of the decision on traffic, and the commission is considering ordering a traffic report. If, under the circumstances, it is reasonably foreseeable that TJKM will be awarded additional work because of the decision, Mr. Springer should not participate in the decision if the materiality tests of Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) are met.

The exceptions contained in Regulation 18700.1(b)(4) would allow Mr. Springer to work on projects for Dublin. For the reasons stated above, as a commissioner he would not be able to make or participate in making a decision concerning his own work. Moreover, he may not have any contact with the agency regarding his work except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the report. Please review the Holbert advice letter (Advice Letter No. I 90-080, copy enclosed) for details regarding permissible communication with city staff.

I trust this letter adequately addresses your questions. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

QUESTION: As a planning commissioner, may Mr. Springer participate in decisions where either he or his employer has prepared a traffic analysis or some other report for the city?

---

¹, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: Mr. Springer may not make, participate in making, or attempt to influence a governmental decision that will have a material financial effect on TJKM, his source of income. He may prepare traffic reports if he does not participate in a decision regarding the report and has no contact with the agency regarding the report except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the report.
